name: Cardiovascular System
use: General information only — research/educational, not medical advice.
what: The heart and blood vessels that pump blood, delivering oxygen and nutrients to every cell and carrying away waste.
how: The heart beats ~100,000 times a day, pushing blood through arteries, capillaries and veins in a closed loop.

name: Respiratory System
use: General information only — research/educational, not medical advice.
what: The lungs and airways that bring oxygen into the blood and remove carbon dioxide with every breath.
how: Air reaches tiny alveoli where oxygen crosses into the blood and CO₂ crosses out, driven by the diaphragm.

name: Nervous System
use: General information only — research/educational, not medical advice.
what: The brain, spinal cord and nerves — the body's electrical control network for thought, movement and sensation.
how: Billions of neurons fire electrochemical signals, coordinating everything from reflexes to memory and emotion.

name: Digestive System
use: General information only — research/educational, not medical advice.
what: The gut that breaks food into usable energy and nutrients, and home to the microbiome that shapes health.
how: Enzymes and acids break food down; the small intestine absorbs nutrients while gut bacteria aid digestion and immunity.

name: Endocrine System
use: General information only — research/educational, not medical advice.
what: The hormone glands (thyroid, adrenals, pancreas and more) that regulate energy, mood, growth and metabolism.
how: Glands release hormones into the blood as chemical messengers that tune organs and processes body-wide.

name: Immune System
use: General information only — research/educational, not medical advice.
what: The body's defence network that detects and destroys pathogens and abnormal cells, and remembers past threats.
how: White blood cells, antibodies and lymph nodes identify invaders and mount targeted, memory-forming responses.

name: Kidneys & Liver (filtration)
use: General information only — research/educational, not medical advice.
what: The body's natural filters — kidneys clean the blood and balance fluids; the liver detoxifies and processes nutrients.
how: Kidneys filter waste into urine and balance salts; the liver neutralises toxins and manages fats, sugars and proteins.

name: Musculoskeletal System
use: General information only — research/educational, not medical advice.
what: Bones, muscles and joints that give the body structure, movement and protection, and store minerals.
how: Muscles contract against the bony levers of the skeleton; joints allow motion while bone stores calcium and makes blood.

name: Type O Blood
use: General information only — research/educational, not medical advice.
what: The most common blood type. O-negative is the 'universal donor' — usable in emergencies for almost anyone.
how: Type O red cells carry no A or B antigens, so other immune systems don't attack them; O-negative also lacks Rh.

name: Type A Blood
use: General information only — research/educational, not medical advice.
what: A common blood type carrying the A antigen; can donate to A and AB recipients.
how: Red cells display the A antigen and the plasma carries anti-B antibodies, defining compatibility.

name: Type B Blood
use: General information only — research/educational, not medical advice.
what: A less common blood type carrying the B antigen; can donate to B and AB recipients.
how: Red cells display the B antigen and the plasma carries anti-A antibodies.

name: Type AB Blood
use: General information only — research/educational, not medical advice.
what: The rarest main type. AB-positive is the 'universal recipient' — able to receive any ABO blood.
how: Red cells carry both A and B antigens and the plasma has neither antibody, so most donor blood is accepted.

name: Rh Factor (+ / −)
use: General information only — research/educational, not medical advice.
what: A protein that's either present (Rh+) or absent (Rh−) on red cells, added to your ABO type. Critical in pregnancy.
how: The RhD antigen determines +/−; an Rh− mother carrying an Rh+ baby may need treatment to prevent immune reaction.

name: DNA & The Genome
use: General information only — research/educational, not medical advice.
what: The molecular instruction book in every cell — 3 billion letters of code that build and run the body.
how: DNA's four bases (A,T,C,G) pair in a double helix; sequences called genes are read to build proteins.

name: Genes & Inherited Traits
use: General information only — research/educational, not medical advice.
what: Units of DNA passed from parents that influence everything from eye colour to disease risk.
how: You inherit one copy of each gene from each parent; dominant and recessive combinations shape traits.

name: Epigenetics
use: General information only — research/educational, not medical advice.
what: How lifestyle, diet and environment switch genes on or off without changing the DNA code itself.
how: Chemical tags (like methylation) sit on top of DNA, dialling gene activity up or down — some are even heritable.

name: Genetic Testing
use: General information only — research/educational, not medical advice.
what: Reading parts of your DNA to reveal ancestry, carrier status and risk for certain conditions.
how: A saliva or blood sample is sequenced or gene-chipped and compared against known variants.

name: Food Allergies
use: General information only — research/educational, not medical advice.
what: An immune overreaction to normally harmless foods (nuts, shellfish, dairy) that can range from mild to severe.
how: The immune system wrongly flags a food protein as a threat and releases histamine, causing the reaction.

name: Environmental Allergies
use: General information only — research/educational, not medical advice.
what: Reactions to airborne triggers like pollen, dust mites, mould or pet dander — hay fever and asthma.
how: Inhaled allergens trigger histamine release in the airways and eyes, causing sneezing, congestion and itch.

name: Histamine Intolerance
use: General information only — research/educational, not medical advice.
what: A build-up of histamine the body struggles to clear, causing flushing, headaches or gut upset after certain foods.
how: Low activity of the DAO enzyme that breaks down histamine lets it accumulate from aged or fermented foods.

name: Autoimmune Sensitivities
use: General information only — research/educational, not medical advice.
what: Conditions where the immune system mistakenly attacks the body's own tissues (thyroid, joints, gut).
how: Loss of self-tolerance leads immune cells to target healthy tissue; triggers are often genetic plus environmental.

name: Chronic Fatigue
use: General information only — research/educational, not medical advice.
what: Persistent, unexplained exhaustion not fixed by rest — sometimes with no clear single cause found in tests.
how: Likely involves a mix of mitochondrial, immune, hormonal and nervous-system factors; research is ongoing.

name: Nutrient Deficiency Fatigue
use: General information only — research/educational, not medical advice.
what: Tiredness caused by low iron, B12, vitamin D or magnesium — one of the most common reversible causes.
how: These nutrients are needed to carry oxygen and make cellular energy; low levels sap stamina and focus.

name: Thyroid & Hormonal Fatigue
use: General information only — research/educational, not medical advice.
what: An under- or over-active thyroid, or hormone imbalance, that slows or destabilises the body's energy regulation.
how: Thyroid hormones set the body's metabolic pace; too little leaves everything sluggish, including energy.

name: Blood-Sugar Swings
use: General information only — research/educational, not medical advice.
what: Energy crashes driven by spikes and dips in blood glucose from diet, common yet often overlooked.
how: Refined carbs spike glucose then insulin overshoots, causing a slump; steadier meals smooth the curve.

name: Sleep Debt & Circadian Drift
use: General information only — research/educational, not medical advice.
what: Accumulated lost sleep and a body clock out of sync with daylight — a leading cause of everyday tiredness.
how: Too little deep and REM sleep, or late light exposure, desynchronises the circadian rhythm that times energy.

name: Sleep Optimisation
use: General information only — research/educational, not medical advice.
what: The single highest-leverage health lever — protecting deep, regular sleep to restore body and brain.
how: Consistent timing, morning light, cool dark rooms and no late caffeine deepen restorative sleep stages.

name: Hydration & Electrolytes
use: General information only — research/educational, not medical advice.
what: Keeping water and mineral balance right — even mild dehydration cuts energy, focus and mood.
how: Water plus sodium, potassium and magnesium keep cells and nerves working; needs rise with heat and activity.

name: Whole-Food Nutrition
use: General information only — research/educational, not medical advice.
what: Fuelling the body with whole foods, protein, fibre and micronutrients rather than processed calories.
how: Nutrient-dense food supplies the building blocks and cofactors every system needs to run and repair.

name: Fasting & Metabolic Rest
use: General information only — research/educational, not medical advice.
what: Giving the digestive system regular breaks, studied for metabolic health and cellular clean-up (autophagy).
how: Longer gaps between meals lower insulin and may trigger autophagy, the cell's recycling of damaged parts.

name: Natural Detox Pathways
use: General information only — research/educational, not medical advice.
what: Supporting the body's own cleansing organs — liver, kidneys, gut and skin — rather than fad 'detox' products.
how: Fibre, water, sleep and movement help the liver and kidneys process and clear waste efficiently and safely.

name: Breathwork & Stress Reset
use: General information only — research/educational, not medical advice.
what: Using slow, controlled breathing to shift the nervous system out of stress and into recovery.
how: Slow exhales activate the vagus/parasympathetic 'rest and digest' response, lowering heart rate and stress.

name: AI Symptom Pattern Detection
use: General information only — research/educational, not medical advice.
what: A future module that cross-links symptoms, labs, sleep, diet and genetics to spot patterns humans miss — pointing toward causes and, one day, cures.
how: As local AI grows, it can correlate huge personal and population datasets to surface hidden links behind 'unexplained' conditions.

name: AI-Assisted Cure Discovery
use: General information only — research/educational, not medical advice.
what: A research vision: AI connecting biology, chemistry and clinical data to propose and prioritise new treatments faster than trial-and-error.
how: Models trained across molecular and outcome data can suggest candidate mechanisms and compounds for humans to test.
